perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to comply with all United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to comply with some United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all' to 'some' introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance regarding export regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: EAR requires compliance with *all* applicable export control regulations. Changing 'all' to 'some' allows the Recipient to selectively comply, which violates the requirement for full adherence to export control laws.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The change from "all" to "some" United States export controls weakens the original obligation. The Law Explanation states that EAR requires compliance with *all* applicable export control regulations, and changing 'all' to 'some' allows selective compliance, violating the requirement for full adherence to export control laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from 15 CFR Part 732, outlines steps for using the EAR. Although it doesn't explicitly state 'all', the comprehensive nature of export control regulations suggests full compliance, which is undermined by the change to 'some'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreem ent shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California, except when federal law preempts.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'except when federal law preempts' can create uncertainty if not specified which federal law applies, conflicting with standard contract law interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution, Article VI
perturbation.law_explanation: While federal law *does* preempt state law under the Supremacy Clause, broadly stating 'except when federal law preempts' without specifying the applicable federal law creates ambiguity that contradicts established principles of contractual interpretation. This implies uncertainty when the contract should operate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by broadly stating 'except when federal law preempts' without specifying the relevant federal law. This contradicts the original text's clear and direct governing law provision and creates uncertainty, aligning with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because it deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable. The Supremacy Clause, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, does establish federal preemption, but merely referencing it without specific application creates ambiguity that could undermine contract enforceability. The original text ensured clear application of California law, and the change introduces an element that weakens the original contract's stance.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to return to the Company immediately upon the Company’s written request all Confidential Information, including but not limited to all computer programs, documentation, notes, plans, drawings, and copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to return to the Company after a reasonable period upon the Company’s written request all Confidential Information, including but not limited to all computer programs, documentation, notes, plans, drawings, and copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'immediately' with 'after a reasonable period' introduces a subjective delay, making enforcement less certain and potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws requiring prompt action.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets through injunctive relief and damages. By changing 'immediately' to 'after a reasonable period,' the contract weakens the protection of confidential information because it does not guarantee the immediate return which hinders the trade secret owner's capacity to act immediately to safeguard it's Trade Secret.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'immediately' to 'after a reasonable period' introduces ambiguity regarding the timely return of confidential information, potentially weakening trade secret protection. The Law Explanation supports that this change hinders the trade secret owner's ability to act immediately to safeguard their trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, implying a legal framework for protecting trade secrets. By allowing a 'reasonable period' for return, the changed text contradicts the need for immediate action to prevent misappropriation, as the time frame is now subjective and open to interpretation, weakening the original intent for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient expressly agrees not to use the Confidential Information for purposes other than those necessary to consider the possibility of entering into a business relationship with the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees not to use the Confidential Information for purposes other than general informational purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'those necessary to consider the possibility of entering into a business relationship with the Company' to 'general informational purposes' broadens the permitted use of confidential information, potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes using them for purposes other than those explicitly agreed upon. The original contract limits use to exploring a business relationship, which provides better protection, while 'general informational purposes' is far broader and doesn't prevent the recipient from using the information in a way that harms the Company's interests, thereby infringing upon trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the permitted use of confidential information from 'those necessary to consider the possibility of entering into a business relationship with the Company' to 'general informational purposes.' This contradicts trade secret law because it allows the recipient to use the information in ways not explicitly agreed upon, potentially harming the Company's interests. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' which the original text protects against. The modified text weakens this protection. The original limits use to exploring a business relationship, providing better protection.
